message:
perf(sync): skip JSONL rewrite when dirty markers don't change content

Before this change, `auto_flush` always rewrote the full JSONL file
when ANY dirty marker was set, even when the proposed replacement
lines were byte-identical to the corresponding existing lines on
disk. The read-modify-rewrite path through read_jsonl_lines_by_id
+ write_jsonl_lines_atomically does an fsync + atomic rename every
time, which shows up as noise in both IO profiles and file-watcher
churn for workflows that touch metadata without actually mutating
content.

Add a scan-only fast path in try_incremental_auto_flush() that
runs before the full load-and-rewrite. It applies only when the
only pending operation is replace-in-place (removed_hash_ids is
empty and replacement_lines is non-empty):

- scan_existing_jsonl_replacements() streams the on-disk JSONL
  through a BufReader once, tracking:
    * exported_count — total non-empty lines seen
    * changed — true if ANY replacement_lines entry differs
      byte-for-byte from its matching on-disk line
    * all_replacements_seen — whether every replacement ID was
      observed (a missing ID means we'd need to append, which
      the scan path cannot do)
    * sorted_by_id — whether the existing file is already sorted
      by ID (required before we commit to an in-place rewrite,
      since we don't re-sort in this path)
  Duplicate IDs still fail the same way as the full load path,
  preserving the existing invariant.

- try_write_existing_jsonl_replacements_atomically() wraps the
  scan and dispatches:
    * Unchanged { exported_count } — all replacement lines match
      on disk; clear dirty markers via finalize_incremental_auto_flush
      with no content-hash update, return AutoFlushResult::default()
      (flushed=false). This is the new no-op fast path.
    * Written { content_hash, exported_count } — at least one
      replacement differs AND the file was already sorted, so we
      stream-rewrite line-by-line via
      write_existing_jsonl_replacements_atomically() without ever
      loading the full HashMap<String, String> into memory: open
      a temp file under the same parent, walk the existing file
      through BufReader::read_line, emit either the replacement
      or the original line, hash/count as we go, sync_all + durable
      rename, and clear the dirty markers with the computed hash.
      The temp file is guarded by TempFileGuard and its path is
      validated via validate_sync_path_with_external +
      require_safe_sync_overwrite_path, so the sandboxing from the
      full rewrite path still applies.
    * Fallback — returned when replacement coverage is incomplete
      OR the file wasn't sorted, so the classic load-mutate-rewrite
      path runs and handles the uncommon case.

- The legacy path is retained and now explicitly re-checks `changed`
  against its own HashMap<String, String> replacements so removals
  still take the full rewrite path (scan path bails early if
  removed_hash_ids is non-empty). The ExportConfig construction is
  hoisted above the scan path since both branches need it.

New unit test: `test_auto_flush_clears_byte_identical_dirty_marker_without_rewrite`
creates an issue, flushes, records the on-disk bytes, marks the issue
dirty via `replace_dirty_issue_marker` without changing its serialized
content, and asserts that the second auto_flush returns
flushed=false, clears all dirty IDs, AND leaves the file bytes
identical to the pre-flush snapshot.

Update tests/repro_auto_flush_inefficiency.rs: rewrite the header
comment and the #[ignore] reason to match the new reality. The
optimization IS now implemented, but `update_issue` always advances
`updated_at`, so a change-then-revert pair is still a real JSONL
content change — the integration surface has no supported way to
mark an issue dirty while preserving byte-identity, so the repro
stays ignored as a spec with a clearer explanation.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
